title: Today's Tech Brief: The Download: keeping up with AI, and the future of IVF
summary: A look at the latest developments in AI and its impact on various industries
imageAlt: Image from source article for The Download: keeping up with AI, and the future of IVF
imageCaption: The Download: keeping up with AI, and the future of IVF
imageSourceTitle: The Download: keeping up with AI, and the future of IVF

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. We're kicking off with a newsletter from MIT Technology Review, called The Download. It provides a daily dose of what's going on in the world of technology. This edition focuses on AI and its applications, including the future of IVF. I find it interesting how AI is being explored for its potential to improve medical treatments and make them more efficient.
The AI Fight at The New York Times

Next, I see that unionized tech employees at The New York Times are pushing back against the company's use of AI tools to monitor employee performance. My read is that this is a growing pain point as AI becomes more prevalent in the workplace. The union claims the company is violating their contract by using these tools. It's a tricky situation - AI can be useful for evaluating performance, but it's also a sensitive topic when it comes to employee privacy and job security.

The intersection of AI and healthcare is also making waves in the world of IVF. An article from MIT Technology Review explores how AI might change the game for fertility treatments. What stands out to me is the potential for AI to improve success rates and make the process more efficient. It's heartening to see technology being used to help people build their families.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
